summary:
The most common extraradicular microorganism associated with dental infections such as pericoronitis and apical abscesses is typically an Actinomyces species due to its frequent detection in affected areas of the mouth. These bacteria thrive well within complex anatomies including roots and surrounding soft tissue structures where other less prevalent microbes struggle to survive or proliferate effectively under similar conditions. To summarize concisely for your query,

The primary extraradicular microorganism identified across multiple sources is *Actinomyces*, which frequently causes issues related to tooth infection by thriving both inside teeth and around them through processes involving inflammation and pus formation typical seen clinically. This aligns closely with what has been observed repeatedly among patients presenting symptoms indicative thereof at clinics worldwide over time periods studied extensively via research methods
